      Polish structural before prose — do not polish text that will soon be cut
    how
    • ·
      3 output files: prd.md + addendum.md + .memlog.md
    • ·
      Common next steps: bmad-ux · bmad-architecture · bmad-create-epics-and-stories
Skills
bmad-prd(unified v6.10.0 — CP / VP / EP)
Frameworks
FR/NFR taxonomyUser StoriesAcceptance Criteria (Gherkin)MoSCoWSMART metricsPersona modeling
Output Formats
Markdown (sharded)YAML frontmatter
Tools · MCP
facilitator modeadvanced-elicitationparty-modebmad-help
IN
product-brief.mdprfaq-{project}.md
OUT
prd.md
02 · B
UX Design
Runs after PRD, before Architecture: user journey, IA, wireframes, interaction, accessibility.
managed byUX Expert
  1. 01
    Load PRD & context
    Read PRD, understand users, FRs and NFRs relevant to UX.
    requirements analysis
    what

    AI loads PRD and extracts UX-relevant FRs + NFRs before starting design.

    • 1
      AI loads prd.md, extracts personas + FRs with UI surface
    • 2
      Identify NFRs that constrain UX
      • Performance — p95 <200ms rules out heavy animations
      • Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA requires focus states, contrast ratios
      • Responsive — mobile-first if mobile traffic >50%
    • 3
      Output: FR → UX implication table (e.g.: FR-03 'user uploads file' → drag-drop + progress + error handling)
    why
    • ·
      UX is not separate from requirements — NFR constraints rule out certain design patterns
    • ·
      Starting with requirements load prevents sketching wireframes that need to be revised
    • ·
      Performance NFR in particular: 200ms threshold rules out long skeleton screens
    how
    • ·
      Requirements traceability: FR → UX needs mapping table
    • ·
      AI highlights NFRs that constrain design (mark with ⚠️ in notes)
    • ·
      Personas extracted → primary persona drives primary flow
  2. 02
    User journey mapping
    Map the journey for each persona — touchpoints, emotions, pain points.
    journey mappingJTBD
    what

    AI maps user journey for each persona: touchpoints, emotions, pain points, opportunities.

    • 1
      Journey map 5 stages
      • Awareness — where users hear about product
      • Consideration — evaluation, comparison
      • Onboarding — signup → first meaningful action
      • Regular use — habitual engagement pattern
      • Advocacy — retention, referral, renewal
    • 2
      Each stage: touchpoints + emotion (frustrated/curious/delighted) + pain + opportunity
    • 3
      Flag moments of truth — transitions where users most likely to drop off
    why
    • ·
      Static screens hide flow — journey reveals transitions where UX problems live
    • ·
      Most dropoff happens at transitions, not at the screens themselves
    • ·
      Peak-end rule: design peak delight moment + strong end moment
    how
    • ·
      Journey mapping methodology with an emotion curve
    • ·
      Transitions tagged with 'danger level' — high = prioritize design attention
    • ·
      One journey per persona — don't merge (different people, different paths)
  3. 03
    Information architecture
    Information structure, navigation, sitemap, content hierarchy.
    card sortingsitemap
    what

    AI defines Information Architecture: sitemap, navigation hierarchy, content groupings.

    • 1
      Output of this step
      • Sitemap — all screens/pages and relationships
      • Navigation structure — top-nav / sidebar / breadcrumb decision
      • Content taxonomy — categories, labels, search terms
    • 2
      AI uses card sorting to discover user's mental model
    • 3
      Validate: can the user find feature X in ≤3 clicks?
    why
    • ·
      Bad IA = user gets lost even when the product has enough features
    • ·
      Research: if users can't find it in 3 clicks, they assume the feature doesn't exist
    • ·
      Defining IA upfront = no costly rework after mockups are polished
    how
    • ·
      Card sorting: open (user groups) or closed (user fits into pre-set)
    • ·
      Tree testing: validate IA with real tasks
    • ·
      Output: sitemap diagram + nav structure decision rationale
  4. 04
    Wireframes (low-fi)
    Sketch the main screens with layout and basic structure.
    low-fi prototyping
    what

    AI sketches low-fidelity wireframes for 5-10 main screens — gray boxes, no color.

    • 1
      Mobile-first: sketch mobile viewport first, desktop after
    • 2
      Gray rectangles + placeholder text — focus on layout + hierarchy, not aesthetics
    • 3
      Per screen annotations
      • Primary action — most important thing user does here
      • Navigation — entry/exit points
      • Content blocks — type, priority, size estimate
    why
    • ·
      Lo-fi because high-fi too early = team debates colors instead of debating flow
    • ·
      Lo-fi cheap to discard — wrong → fix in 30 minutes, not 3 days
    • ·
      Mobile-first reveals priorities: limited space forces what is actually important to surface
    how
    • ·
      Crazy 8s technique: sketch 8 variations in 8 minutes before committing
    • ·
      Gray only — no brand colors, no real images, no real text
    • ·
      Annotation mandatory — no annotation = design intent cannot be shared
  5. 05
    Interaction patterns
    Define patterns (form, search, filter, modal...) and micro-interactions.
    interaction designmicro-interactions
    what

    AI defines interaction patterns — standardize behavior across product.

    • 1
      Core patterns to define
      • Forms — validation timing (on-blur vs on-submit), error display, inline vs summary
      • Search — autocomplete, filters, results pagination
      • Modals — when to use, dismissible via ESC/backdrop, focus trap
      • Notifications — toast / inline / system / email trigger conditions
      • Loading states — skeleton / spinner / progress bar — when each
    • 2
      Each pattern: trigger condition + behavior + edge cases
    why
    • ·
      Pattern reuse = consistency — predictability builds trust
    • ·
      Define once → all forms behave same way; user learns once, applies everywhere
    • ·
      Inconsistent patterns = cognitive load on every screen the user must re-learn
    how
    • ·
      Reference Material Design / Apple HIG / Carbon for industry-standard patterns
    • ·
      Custom patterns only when the product has a strong reason — document rationale
    • ·
      Pattern library output feeds developer component specs in step 06
  6. 06
    Component specs
    Describe component types, states, variants — prepare for dev.
    atomic design
    what

    AI lists component specs — each component has states + variants + behavior.

    • 1
      Atom-level components by Atomic Design
      • Atoms — Button, Input, Label, Icon, Badge
      • Molecules — FormField, SearchBar, Card, MenuItem
      • Organisms — Header, DataTable, Modal, Sidebar
    • 2
      Each component spec
      • States — default / hover / active / disabled / error / loading
      • Variants — primary / secondary / destructive / ghost
      • Accessibility — ARIA role, keyboard, focus visible
    why
    • ·
      Component spec is the contract between UX and Dev
    • ·
      Underspec → dev guess → inconsistency across screens
    • ·
      Atomic Design (Brad Frost): build small, compose large — reusable and maintainable
    how
    • ·
      Atomic Design methodology: atoms → molecules → organisms → templates → pages
    • ·
      Each component: visual spec + behavior spec + a11y spec + code stub
    • ·
      Export to Figma component library if the team uses Figma
  7. 07
    Responsive design
    Breakpoints, mobile/tablet/desktop behaviors, adaptive patterns.
    mobile-firstprogressive enhancement
    what

    AI defines responsive behavior: breakpoints + per-breakpoint layout decisions.

    • 1
      Standard breakpoints
      • Mobile — <640px: single column, bottom nav
      • Tablet — 640-1024px: 2 column, side nav optional
      • Desktop — >1024px: multi-column, expanded nav
    • 2
      Per-breakpoint decisions
      • Navigation pattern changes (bottom nav → side nav → top nav)
      • Content priority (mobile hides secondary actions)
      • Component reflow (card stack vs grid)
    why
    • ·
      70% web traffic mobile — mobile-first is not an afterthought
    • ·
      Mobile-first vs desktop-first determines downstream code structure
    • ·
      Explicit breakpoints prevent developers from picking random values (768? 800? 1024?)
    how
    • ·
      Progressive enhancement: start mobile, add capabilities for larger screens
    • ·
      Content priority per breakpoint: what shows, what hides, what reflows
    • ·
      Test on real devices — DevTools doesn't capture touch behavior
  8. 08
    Accessibility
    WCAG 2.1 AA: keyboard nav, screen reader, contrast, focus states.
    WCAG 2.1ARIA
    what

    AI specifies accessibility per WCAG 2.1 AA — not a nice-to-have.

    • 1
      Core WCAG 2.1 AA requirements
      • Contrast — 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
      • Keyboard nav — all interactive elements reachable via Tab
      • Screen reader — meaningful labels, alt text, ARIA where needed
      • Focus visible — clear focus indicator on all elements
      • Error identification — errors described in text, not only color
      • Motion — prefers-reduced-motion respected
    why
    • ·
      Legal requirement in many markets: ADA (US), EAA (EU 2025)
    • ·
      Cost of retrofit 10x cost of build-in
    • ·
      Accessibility improvements help all users — SEO, keyboard power users, mobile
    how
    • ·
      WCAG 2.1 AA checklist + ARIA patterns
    • ·
      Tools: aXe DevTools, Lighthouse a11y audit
    • ·
      Manual test: keyboard-only navigation + screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver)
  9. 09
    Design system references
    Link to the design system / tokens, or recommend creating one.
    design tokens
    what

    AI references or proposes building a design system with semantic tokens.

    • 1
      Design tokens — W3C standard
      • Color — semantic naming: color.bg.primary not color.blue.500
      • Typography — scale: xs/sm/md/lg/xl, line-heights, weights
      • Spacing — 4px base grid, named scale: 1/2/3/4/6/8/12/16...
      • Shadow, radius, motion — consistent values
    • 2
      1 token change propagates everywhere — brand refresh = update 20 tokens, not 200 screens
    why
    • ·
      Without design system: every screen reinvents wheels
    • ·
      Token-based approach lets 1 change propagate everywhere
    • ·
      Critical for Level 3-4 projects with multiple feature teams
    how
    • ·
      W3C Design Tokens standard format
    • ·
      Reference: Tailwind, Material, Carbon, Polaris — pick closest to team's stack
    • ·
      Semantic naming: describes role, not appearance
  10. 10
    Finalize — save DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md
    Save two-spine output; hand off to Architecture.
    specification
    what

    AI saves two-spine output and clearly hands off to Architecture.

    • 1
      DESIGN.md — visual + interaction spine
      • Information Architecture — sitemap + nav structure
      • Wireframes — embedded or linked
      • Interaction patterns — pattern library
      • Component specs — states + variants
      • Responsive behavior — breakpoint table
      • Design system — tokens reference
    • 2
      EXPERIENCE.md — user journey spine
      • User journeys — per persona
      • Accessibility — WCAG checklist
      • Edge / empty / error states — per screen
    • 3
      AI invokes bmad-help → suggest Architecture (Phase 3) next
    why
    • ·
      UX spec is an input to Architecture — component list informs frontend framework choice
    • ·
      Accessibility spec informs library choice (a11y-first vs not)
    • ·
      Interaction patterns inform state management approach
    how
    • ·
      Handoff format standard BMAD — frontmatter complete with stepsCompleted
    • ·
      Wireframes: embed as ASCII/text description if Figma is not available
    • ·
      bmad-help arg='ux-design' → recommend next: architecture
Skills
bmad-uxbmad-agent-tech-writer (mermaid intent)
Frameworks
User Journey MappingInformation ArchitectureWireframingWCAG 2.1 AAResponsive breakpointsDesign Systems (Material 3 · HIG · Carbon · Polaris · Tailwind)
Output Formats
Markdown specMermaid diagrams
Tools · MCP
advanced-elicitationparty-modebmad-help
IN
prd.md
OUT
DESIGN.mdEXPERIENCE.md
03
Solutioning
Decide how to build · Break work into stories
Required
03 · A
Architecture
Intent-based architecture workflow (v6.10.0) — lean spine pattern; ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md as source of truth. Coaching path default; memlog append-only; mandatory Reviewer Gate before handoff.
managed byArchitect
  1. 01
    Init & load PRD
    Read PRD and UX, identify the most important architecture drivers.
    requirements traceability
    what

    AI loads PRD + UX artifacts (DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md) and identifies top architecture drivers.

    • 1
      AI extracts from PRD
      • Top 3-5 NFRs with the highest architectural impact
      • Key FRs with high architectural coupling (e.g.: real-time sync, file upload, payments)
      • Compliance constraints from step 05 of PRD
    • 2
      Rank drivers: performance / security / scalability / maintainability
    • 3
      Output: driver list with justification — this is the 'north star' for all subsequent decisions
    why
    • ·
      Not all NFRs are equal — 'fast' and '99.99% uptime' have completely different implications
    • ·
      Top 3-5 drivers focus architecture effort, avoiding over-engineering things that don't matter
    • ·
      Without explicit drivers, the team debates style preferences instead of tradeoffs
    how
    • ·
      Quality attribute taxonomy: performance / availability / security / modifiability / testability
    • ·
      Drivers ranked by business impact if violated
    • ·
      ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method) light-version
  2. 02
    High-level architecture
    Draw the overall system diagram, identify components and boundaries.
    C4 modelsystem design
    what

    AI draws high-level system architecture following the C4 model.

    • 1
      C4 model — 4 levels
      • Context diagram — system boundary + external actors (users, 3rd party systems)
      • Container diagram — major deployable units (web app, API, DB, queue, CDN)
      • Component diagram — major components within each container
      • Code diagram — optional, only for critical paths
    • 2
      Sweet spot: Container diagram is the most useful artifact for team alignment
    • 3
      Annotate data flows with: protocol + auth method + sync/async
    why
    • ·
      C4 Context = helps all stakeholders (including non-tech) understand system boundaries
    • ·
      C4 Container = helps engineers understand major building blocks and dependencies
    • ·
      Layered abstraction: dig down only when needed — not overwhelming
    how
    • ·
      C4 model by Simon Brown — text-based (PlantUML/Mermaid) or diagram
    • ·
      Annotations mandatory: unlabeled arrows = ambiguous coupling
    • ·
      Container diagram stable through refactors — code changes but containers rarely change
  3. 03
    Tech stack decision
    Choose language, framework, DB, infra — record ADRs (Architecture Decision Records).
    ADRtrade-off analysis
    what

    AI decide tech stack — each major decision is one ADR.

    • 1
      Standard ADR format (Michael Nygard)
      • Title — 'ADR-001: Use PostgreSQL for primary data store'
      • Status — Proposed / Accepted / Superseded
      • Context — what problem are we solving
      • Decision — what we decided
      • Consequences — tradeoffs accepted
      • Alternatives — what we rejected and why
    • 2
      Each major decision: language, framework, database, cache, queue, infra, observability
    why
    • ·
      ADRs record why, not just what — six months later the team does not re-debate
    • ·
      'Because the team knows it' is an input, not a decision reason
    • ·
      Documented alternatives prevent future revisiting of rejected options
    how
    • ·
      Trade-off analysis: score alternatives by NFR drivers
    • ·
      Each surviving decision becomes an AD-n entry inside ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md, not a separate file
    • ·
      Inherited/parent ADs are binding and read-only; rationale is captured in .memlog.md
  4. 04
    Data model
    Design entities, relationships, schema, and migration strategy.
    ERDevent sourcing
    what

    AI designs the data model: entities, relationships, schema, indexes, migration strategy.

    • 1
      Data model artifacts
      • Entity list — major business objects (User, Order, Product...)
      • ERD — relationships: 1-1, 1-N, N-M
      • Schema — key fields, types, constraints, indexes
      • Sensitive data — PII fields tagged, encryption strategy
    • 2
      Migration strategy
      • Zero-downtime: expand-contract pattern (blue-green migrations)
      • Maintenance window: simpler but requires downtime
      • Decision depends on SLA NFRs
    why
    • ·
      Data model changes are the most expensive after shipping — front-load them
    • ·
      Schema design exposes implicit business rules the PRD does not articulate ('Can a user have multiple addresses?')
    • ·
      Migration strategy must be decided upfront — retrofitting zero-downtime behavior is very complex
    how
    • ·
      ERD diagram for relational data; event sourcing when audit + time-travel are needed
    • ·
      Index strategy: query-driven (YAGNI — don't add indexes speculatively)
    • ·
      PII handling: encrypt at rest, pseudonymize in logs, access control
  5. 05
    API design
    REST/GraphQL/gRPC, endpoints, authentication, versioning, contracts.
    RESTOpenAPI
    what

    AI defines API design: style, endpoints, auth, versioning, contracts.

    • 1
      API style decision
      • REST — resource-oriented, stateless, good for CRUD
      • GraphQL — flexible queries, good for complex data graphs
      • gRPC — binary, streaming, good for internal microservices
      • Event-driven — async, good for decoupling and scale
    • 2
      Auth strategy: OAuth 2.0 / API keys / JWT — pick based on client types
    • 3
      Contract artifacts
      • OpenAPI 3.0 spec for REST
      • SDL (Schema Definition Language) for GraphQL
      • .proto files for gRPC
    why
    • ·
      API contract is the most-coupled interface — clients depend on shape
    • ·
      REST vs GraphQL mismatch between epics = integration nightmare (per the Why Solutioning doc)
    • ·
      Versioning strategy: decide once, all teams follow — URL path vs header
    how
    • ·
      REST best practices: resource-oriented, proper HTTP verbs, consistent naming
    • ·
      Versioning: URL path (/v1/) simpler and more visible
    • ·
      Contract-first: write OpenAPI/SDL before writing code
  6. 06
    Integration patterns
    External services, message queues, webhooks, event patterns.
    event-drivenhexagonal
    what

    AI defines integration patterns for external services.

    • 1
      Each integration must specify
      • Protocol — REST / webhook / event bus / SDK
      • Auth — API key / OAuth / mTLS
      • Failure handling — timeout + retry policy + fallback
      • Rate limiting — throttle strategy
    • 2
      Resilience patterns
      • Circuit Breaker — fail fast when service down
      • Retry with backoff — exponential, with jitter
      • Dead Letter Queue — catch failed messages
    why
    • ·
      Each integration = a new failure mode (3rd party downtime, rate limits, breaking changes)
    • ·
      Circuit breaker prevents cascade failures — critical for high-availability systems
    • ·
      Without retry + DLQ, transient failures = permanent data loss
    how
    • ·
      Hexagonal architecture (ports & adapters) to isolate external dependencies
    • ·
      Specify timeout/retry/fallback for each integration explicitly
    • ·
      Vendor lock-in assessment: what's the cost of switching?
  7. 07
    Security architecture
    Authn/authz, encryption, secrets management, threat model.
    threat modelingSTRIDE
    what

    AI specifies security architecture with a STRIDE threat model.

    • 1
      Auth + AuthZ stack
      • Authentication — OAuth 2.0 / SAML / JWT / OIDC
      • Authorization — RBAC (role-based) / ABAC (attribute-based)
      • Session management — token lifetime, refresh strategy
    • 2
      Data security
      • Encryption at rest — AES-256 for sensitive data
      • Encryption in transit — TLS 1.3 minimum
      • Secrets management — Vault / AWS Secrets Manager / env vars anti-pattern
    • 3
      STRIDE threat model per component
      • Spoofing / Tampering / Repudiation / Info Disclosure / DoS / Elevation
    why
    • ·
      Security retrofit costs 10-100x more than building it in
    • ·
      Threat modeling exposes implicit attack surfaces early
    • ·
      Compliance (SOC2, ISO 27001) requires documented security architecture
    how
    • ·
      STRIDE per component — systematic coverage
    • ·
      OWASP Top 10 minimum checklist
    • ·
      Secrets in code = automatic fail — use secret manager from day 1
  8. 08
    Deployment architecture
    Cloud topology, environments, CI/CD, observability (logs, metrics, traces).
    12-factorobservability
    what

    AI defines deployment architecture: cloud topology + CI/CD + observability.

    • 1
      Infrastructure
      • Cloud topology — regions, AZs, CDN strategy
      • Environments — dev / staging / prod isolation
      • Scaling — horizontal vs vertical, auto-scaling triggers
    • 2
      Observability triad
      • Logs — structured (JSON), centralized, retention policy
      • Metrics — system (CPU/RAM/latency) + business (DAU, revenue)
      • Traces — distributed tracing for request flows
    • 3
      CI/CD pipeline
      • Build → Test → Security scan → Deploy → Smoke test
      • Rollback strategy: blue-green or feature flags
    why
    • ·
      Production failures invisible without observability
    • ·
      Logs = what happened; Metrics = is system healthy; Traces = why slow
    • ·
      3 pillars required before shipping — retrofitting observability painful
    how
    • ·
      12-factor app principles
    • ·
      OpenTelemetry standard for tracing — vendor-neutral
    • ·
      Observability triad per Cindy Sridharan 'Distributed Systems Observability'
  9. 09
    NFR validation
    Map each NFR to an architectural solution, prove it is achieved.
    ATAMquality scenarios
    what

    AI maps each NFR → architectural solution, prove coverage qualitatively.

    • 1
      NFR validation table
      • NFR — description with metric
      • Architecture solution — which component/pattern addresses it
      • Quantified expectation — calculation or reasoning
      • Validation method — load test / chaos test / audit
    • 2
      AI writes 'NFR-01 p95 <200ms → read replica + CDN + Redis cache → expected ~150ms'
    why
    • ·
      Architecture must close-loop with NFRs — do not assume coverage
    • ·
      Without explicit mapping, NFRs become an afterthought discovered when they fail
    • ·
      Quantified expectation enables later verification with actual load tests
    how
    • ·
      ATAM quality scenarios: stimulus / response / measure
    • ·
      Acceptable tradeoffs documented (e.g.: 'traded 20ms latency for stronger consistency')
    • ·
      Gaps surface: NFR with no architectural solution = must address before Phase 4
  10. 10
    Risk analysis
    Technical risks, scaling bottlenecks, vendor lock-in, mitigation plans.
    failure mode analysis
    what

    AI lists technical risks with FMEA scoring.

    • 1
      Each risk has
      • Description — what could go wrong
      • Category — scaling / vendor / skill / deprecation / security
      • FMEA score — Severity × Occurrence × Detection
      • Mitigation — action + owner
      • Monitoring trigger — early warning signal
    why
    • ·
      Architecture risks differ from business risks — surface only via technical analysis
    • ·
      Unknown unknowns only appear under load or vendor pivot — identify early
    • ·
      Front-loading identification reduces 80% of bad surprises in Phase 4
    how
    • ·
      FMEA scoring: Severity (1-10) × Occurrence (1-10) × Detection (1-10)
    • ·
      Top 5 risks by score get mitigation plans
    • ·
      Failure mode analysis: 'What fails first under 10x load?'
    • ·
      Each risk has owner + trigger condition — know exactly when the risk becomes a real issue
  11. 11–16
    Reviewer Gate + Finalize ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
    Run lint_spine.py + parallel subagent review; save ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md + ADRs, suggest Epics & Stories.
    Reviewer GateADR documentation
    what

    Reviewer Gate runs (lint_spine.py + parallel subagents) then AI saves ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md and hands off.

    • 1
      Reviewer Gate
      • uv run lint_spine.py — validates invariants completeness
      • Parallel subagents: breadth-coverage rubrics across all spine sections
      • Gate must pass before handoff — findings returned to architect for resolution
    • 2
      Output file
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md — lean spine: invariants that keep features/epics/stories consistent, with decisions recorded as AD-n entries inside it (no separate ADR files); rationale in .memlog.md
    • 3
      AI invokes bmad-help → suggest Epics & Stories (03·B) next
    why
    • ·
      ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md is the single source of truth — invariants that downstream epics and stories must honour
    • ·
      Reviewer Gate prevents incomplete spines from reaching story decomposition
    • ·
      ADRs immutable: records decision history; each new decision = new ADR
    how
    • ·
      Lean spine pattern: capture only invariants — not full C4; full architecture scope is optional extension
    • ·
      memlog: append-only memlog.py — preserves working memory across sessions
    • ·
      bmad-help auto-invoke — uniform handoff pattern
Skills
bmad-architecture
Frameworks
Lean SpineAD-n decisionsmemlogReviewer Gate
Output Formats
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
Tools · MCP
memlog.pylint_spine.pyadvanced-elicitationparty-modebmad-help
IN
prd.mdDESIGN.md / EXPERIENCE.md
OUT
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
03 · D
Readiness Check
Gate check (v6.10.0) — 6-step sequential. Step 1 interactive only; Steps 2–6 auto. Validates FR coverage, UX alignment, epic quality. Verdict: READY / NEEDS WORK / NOT READY. Severity: Critical / Major / Minor.
managed byPM
  1. 01
    Load planning docs
    Read PRD, architecture, epics, DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md — gather all planning artifacts.
    document review
    what

    John (PM) loads all planning artifacts and builds inventory.

    • 1
      Load documents
      • prd.md + addendum.md + .memlog.md
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
      • epics.md
      • DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md if available
    • 2
      Builds artifact inventory: file list + version + last-updated
    • 3
      Flag missing artifacts before starting review
    why
    • ·
      Readiness check is the gatekeeper before Phase 4
    • ·
      PO ≠ PM — independent reviewer reduces self-confirmation bias
    • ·
      BMAD tip: use different LLM for validation — same LLM finds its own writing consistent
    how
    • ·
      PO profile: skeptical, thorough, user-advocate perspective
    • ·
      Artifact inventory: date comparison (architecture newer than PRD = check for drift)
    • ·
      Missing artifacts = automatic Concerns flag
    • ·
      Version pin: record the commit/hash of each doc to ensure the review uses the correct version
  2. 02
    PRD quality review
    Check completeness, clarity, FRs/NFRs complete and non-contradictory.
    completeness check
    what

    AI reviews PRD: completeness + consistency + FR quality.

    • 1
      Completeness check
      • Every required section present and non-empty?
      • Every FR has ID + title + description + priority + user story?
      • Every NFR has a metric + threshold + measurement method?
      • Exec summary present and covering all major points?
    • 2
      Consistency check
      • Exec summary match body detail?
      • FR-IDs referenced consistently (FR-03 in one place, FR-3 elsewhere = error)?
      • NFRs do not contradict FRs?
    why
    • ·
      PRD bug most expensive when fixed early — propagates downstream to architecture and stories
    • ·
      1 missing FR = 1 missing feature = customer complaint
    • ·
      1 vague NFR = battle between dev and stakeholder post-ship
    how
    • ·
      Template-driven check: section X exists? Content X has substance?
    • ·
      ID consistency regex: normalize all references before check
    • ·
      Output: checklist with PASS/FAIL/CONCERN per item
  3. 03
    Architecture review
    ADRs have clear rationale, NFRs are addressed, risks have mitigations.
    arch review board
    what

    AI reviews Architecture: ADR quality + NFR coverage + risk register.

    • 1
      ADR quality
      • Every ADR has complete Context/Decision/Consequences/Alternatives?
      • Status field set (Proposed/Accepted)?
      • Do all tech stack decisions have ADRs?
    • 2
      NFR coverage
      • Each NFR from PRD has a component/pattern addressing it in the architecture?
      • Does the 99.9% uptime NFR have an HA topology?
      • Does the security NFR have a threat model?
    • 3
      Risk register present? Do the top 5 risks have mitigations?
    why
    • ·
      Architecture review is often skipped — 'trust the architect'
    • ·
      Independent review catches: missing ADR (decision not documented), under-addressed NFR
    • ·
      NFR-architecture gap = failure surprise in production
    how
    • ·
      ATAM-light: each NFR → find addressing component → rate adequacy
    • ·
      Tech stack alignment against team skills check
    • ·
      Vendor lock-in risk graded
  4. 04
    Cohesion check
    PRD ↔ Architecture: every FR has a solution, every decision is grounded in PRD.
    traceability matrix
    what

    AI run cohesion check: PRD ↔ Architecture traceability.

    • 1
      Build traceability matrix
      • Rows = FRs from PRD
      • Cols = architecture components
      • X = component addresses FR
    • 2
      2 types of issues
      • Empty row — FR has no component → architecture incomplete
      • Empty column — component not justified by any FR → over-engineering
    • 3
      Flag all gaps as Blocker if FR = Must priority
    why
    • ·
      Cohesion gap is a silent killer — PRD says 'users upload files', architecture has no FileService
    • ·
      Discovered after shipping = emergency fix, downtime risk
    • ·
      Over-engineering = wasted sprint capacity + maintenance burden
    how
    • ·
      Same traceability matrix technique as 03·B step 01
    • ·
      PO perspective: 'which feature will users miss?' → check corresponding FR row
    • ·
      ADR cross-check: every architecture decision traces back to a PRD requirement?
  5. 05
    Epic quality review
    Validate against best practices: user value, independence, dependencies.
    INVEST validation
    what

    AI reviews epics: INVEST compliance + AC quality + dependency graph.

    • 1
      Per story INVEST check
      • Independent? (no hard block on adjacent story?)
      • Estimable? (dev can estimate without major uncertainty?)
      • Small? (≤3 days dev work?)
      • Testable? (≥2 AC defined?)
    • 2
      AC quality check
      • Stories with 0-1 AC → insufficient spec flag
      • Stories with >7 AC → too large, split flag
      • Gherkin format: Given/When/Then present?
    • 3
      Dependency graph cycle check: A→B→A = circular dependency = blocker
    why
    • ·
      Bad stories at this gate = bad sprint — sprint failure more expensive
    • ·
      Story without clear AC → code no one can verify
    • ·
      Circular dependency → sprint never completes
    how
    • ·
      Automated INVEST scoring: count AC, check size estimate present
    • ·
      Cycle detection: graph traversal (DFS cycle check)
    • ·
      Output: per-story scorecard with specific issues
  6. 06
    Final assessment
    Conclusion: PASS, CONCERNS (minor fixes needed), or FAIL (must go back).
    go/no-gogate review
    what

    AI concludes with a binary verdict and saves the readiness report.

    • 1
      Verdict criteria
      • PASS — 0 Blockers + ≤3 Concerns → ready for Phase 4
      • NEEDS REVIEW — 0 Blockers + >3 Concerns → proceed with documented caveats
      • FAIL — ≥1 Blocker → must rework before Phase 4
    • 2
      Report structure
      • Scorecard — per-section PASS/CONCERNS/FAIL
      • Blockers — must-fix list with owner
      • Concerns — should-fix list with rationale
      • Verdict — go/no-go with reasoning
    why
    • ·
      Binary gate (go/no-go) is the critical output
    • ·
      Without explicit gate, project drifts into Phase 4 with planning gaps → sprint chaos
    • ·
      CONCERNS state can still proceed, but with documented risks — informed decision
    how
    • ·
      Blocker definition: issue that WILL cause Phase 4 failure if not fixed
    • ·
      Concern definition: issue that MIGHT cause problems but manageable
    • ·
      Save: implementation-readiness-report-{date}.md with timestamp and verdict
Skills
bmad-check-implementation-readiness
Frameworks
Cohesion CheckTraceability MatrixGate ReviewQuality Rubric
Output Formats
Markdown (pass/fail gate)
Tools · MCP
party-mode (PM/PO/Architect)bmad-help
IN
prd.mdARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdepics.mdDESIGN.md / EXPERIENCE.md
OUT
implementation-readiness-report-{date}.md
03 · B
Test Design (System Level)
System-level test strategy from architecture, ADRs, and PRD — testability review (controllability, observability, reliability), risk register with P×I scoring across 6 categories, NFR thresholds, and coverage plan. Runs after architecture is complete, before the implementation-readiness gate.
operated byTest Architect (TEA)
  1. 01
    Detect mode & prerequisites
    Determine System-Level vs Epic-Level mode; confirm PRD, ADR, and architecture doc are available; halt with a clear message if required inputs are missing.
    mode detectionprerequisites
  2. 02
    Load context & knowledge base
    Extract tech stack, integration points, and NFRs from inputs; load tiered knowledge fragments just-in-time (core always, extended on-demand) for 40–50% context saving vs full load.
    tiered loadingknowledge base
  3. 03
    Testability review
    Evaluate architecture for controllability (can we set preconditions?), observability (can we verify outcomes?), and reliability (are test results deterministic?); classify ASRs as Actionable or FYI.
    controllabilityobservabilityreliability
  4. 04
    Risk assessment & prioritization
    Score 6 risk categories (TECH/SEC/PERF/DATA/BUS/OPS) with P×I matrix (1–9 scale); assign P0–P3 priority to each test scenario; high-risk items (score ≥ 6) require mandatory mitigation with owner and timeline.
    P×I scoringrisk registerP0–P3
  5. 05
    Generate output documents
    Write test-design-architecture.md (testability concerns + risk mitigations for Dev/Arch teams), test-design-qa.md (coverage plan + execution recipe for QA team), and BMAD handoff document bridging to epic decomposition.
    test-design-architecture.mdBMAD handoff
Skills
bmad-testarch-test-design
Frameworks
P×I Risk Scoring (1–9)6 Risk Categories (TECH/SEC/PERF/DATA/BUS/OPS)Priority P0–P3ATAM Quality Scenarios
Formats
test-design-architecture.mdtest-design-qa.mdtest-design/{project}-handoff.md
Tools · MCP
advanced-elicitationbmad-help
IN
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdprd.md
OUT
test-design-architecture.mdtest-design-qa.mdtest-design/{project}-handoff.md
03 · E
Framework Setup
Initialize a production-ready test framework — directory structure, config, fixtures (mergeTests pattern), Faker data factories, sample tests, and README. Fully autonomous: auto-detects stack (frontend/backend/fullstack) and selects the appropriate framework with no manual scaffolding. Resumable if interrupted.
operated byTest Architect (TEA)
  1. 01
    Preflight checks
    Read config, auto-detect stack from project manifests (package.json, pyproject.toml, pom.xml, go.mod); verify no conflicting framework exists; confirm write permissions; save detected context to progress file.
    stack detectionprerequisites
  2. 02
    Framework selection
    Frontend: default to Playwright, switch to Cypress only if criteria met. Backend: Python→pytest, Java/Kotlin→JUnit 5, Go→go test, C#→xUnit, Ruby→RSpec, Rust→cargo test. Announce selection with rationale.
    PlaywrightCypresspytest
  3. 03
    Scaffold framework
    Generate complete directory tree, framework config, .env.example, fixtures with mergeTests pattern (auto-cleanup hooks built in), Faker-based data factories, sample tests, and helpers. Adaptive execution: parallel agent-team or sequential based on runtime capability.
    mergeTests patternfixturesFaker factories
  4. 04
    Documentation & scripts
    Create tests/README.md covering setup, running, debugging, architecture, best practices, and CI notes; add idiomatic test commands to package.json, Makefile, or pyproject.toml (minimum: "test:e2e" for frontend).
    tests/README.mdnpm scripts
  5. 05
    Validate & summarize
    Check all checklist items (directory structure, config correctness, fixtures/factories, docs/scripts); fix any gaps before marking complete; report framework selected, all artifacts created, and recommended next steps.
    validation checklistcompletion report
Skills
bmad-testarch-framework
Frameworks
PlaywrightCypresspytestJUnit 5go testxUnitRSpecFaker
Formats
playwright.config.ts / cypress.config.tstests/ directory tree.env.exampletests/README.md
Tools · MCP
bmad-help
IN
package.jsonpyproject.toml / pom.xml / go.mod
OUT
framework config filetests/ directory treetests/README.md
03 · C
Epics & Stories
Created AFTER architecture (v6.10.0) — stories reflect technical decisions correctly. 4-step micro-file: validate prerequisites → design epics → generate stories → final validation. UX-DRs are first-class inputs. JIT entity creation.
managed byPM + Architect
  1. 01
    Load PRD + architecture
    Read both to understand requirements and existing technical decisions.
    traceability
    what

    AI loads PRD + ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md and builds FR ↔ component traceability map.

    • 1
      AI loads both documents in parallel
      • prd.md — FRs, NFRs, personas, success metrics
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md — components, data model, API design, patterns
    • 2
      Build traceability matrix
      • Rows = FRs from PRD
      • Cols = architecture components
      • X = 'this component addresses this FR'
    • 3
      Flag gaps: FR has no component (architecture incomplete) + component has no FR (over-engineering)
    why
    • ·
      v6 improvement: stories are created AFTER architecture — stories now reference specific patterns
    • ·
      Stories created before architecture miss tech context → rework mid-sprint
    • ·
      Traceability surfaces gaps BEFORE sprint capacity is committed
    how
    • ·
      Traceability matrix: rows = FRs, cols = components, X = mapping
    • ·
      Empty row = FR uncovered → must address in architecture
    • ·
      Empty col = component not justified → over-engineering, remove or justify
  2. 02
    Group FRs into epics
    Group related FRs into epics — each epic carries independent value.
    capability mapping
    what

    AI groups FRs into epics — each epic is a user-deliverable capability.

    • 1
      AI clusters FRs by user capability, not by technical layer
    • 2
      Validate per epic
      • User-deliverable: can it be demonstrated standalone?
      • Independent value: do users benefit if only this epic ships?
      • Reasonable scope: 2-6 weeks development?
    • 3
      Bad vs good epic naming
      • Bad: 'Backend', 'Frontend' — technical layers are not capabilities
      • Good: 'User onboarding', 'Payment processing', 'Admin dashboard'
    why
    • ·
      Tech-layer epics do not deliver value individually
    • ·
      User-centric epics deliver value per epic ship — stakeholders see progress
    • ·
      Validate 'demo standalone' as the acid test for epic independence
    how
    • ·
      Capability mapping: each FR maps to exactly 1 epic
    • ·
      Epic size check: one epic too large (>6 weeks) → split; too small (<1 week) → merge
    • ·
      Validate against the product vision: each epic must tell a capability story
  3. 03
    Epic ordering
    Determine epic order by dependencies, risk, and value.
    WSJFvalue stream
    what

    AI orders epics by WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First).

    • 1
      WSJF scoring per epic
      • User-Business Value — direct revenue/UX impact (1-10)
      • Time Criticality — cost of delaying (1-10)
      • Risk Reduction — reduces unknowns if done early (1-10)
      • Job Size — estimated weeks (1-10, higher = larger)
      • WSJF = (Value + Criticality + Risk) / Size
    • 2
      High-risk, high-value epic → ship early (fail fast while resources remain)
    • 3
      First epic must be independent (no dependencies) — unblock parallel work
    why
    • ·
      Shipping in the wrong order = waste — first 4 sprints does not deliver demoable value = stakeholder frustration
    • ·
      Risky epics early: if they fail → pivot while still have runway
    • ·
      Value-first: demonstrate value early → stakeholder confidence + feedback
    how
    • ·
      SAFe WSJF framework
    • ·
      Score subjectively if exact data is unavailable — relative ranking is enough
    • ·
      Visualize: table with scores + bar chart so stakeholders can see the rationale
  4. 04
    Story breakdown
    Split epics into stories using INVEST principles.
    INVESTSPIDR
    what

    AI splits each epic into INVEST stories.

    • 1
      INVEST checklist per story
      • Independent — do not block each other when possible
      • Negotiable — scope can be adjusted
      • Valuable — clear user/business benefit
      • Estimable — effort can be estimated
      • Small — 1-3 days of dev work is ideal
      • Testable — AC can be verified
    • 2
      SPIDR splitting patterns when the story is too large
      • Spike — separate exploration/research story
      • Path — split by user journey paths
      • Interface — split by UI vs API vs integration
      • Data — split by data variations
      • Rules — split by business rules
    why
    • ·
      Story too large → uncertainty → wrong estimates → sprint slips
    • ·
      Story too small → overhead (PR review, deploy) > value
    • ·
      INVEST sweet spot: 1-3 days reduces risk and enables fast feedback
    how
    • ·
      INVEST checklist per story — fail any = must split/reshape
    • ·
      SPIDR patterns: most used = Interface split (API vs UI separate stories)
    • ·
      Story size: count by AC — story with >7 AC is usually too large, split
  5. 05
    Acceptance criteria
    Write AC in Given/When/Then format for each story, with enough detail to test.
    BDDGherkin
    what

    AI writes Acceptance Criteria in BDD/Gherkin format — executable specification.

    • 1
      Gherkin format
      • Given — precondition (state before the action)
      • When — action user takes
      • Then — expected outcome (verifiable)
    • 2
      Coverage per story
      • 1+ AC for happy path
      • 1-2 edge cases (boundary values, empty states)
      • 1 error path (what happens when it fails)
    • 3
      3-7 AC per story — under 3 = insufficient spec; over 7 = story too large
    why
    • ·
      Vague AC ('works correctly') → endless debate about done
    • ·
      BDD format forces precondition + trigger + outcome — do not miss edge cases
    • ·
      Executable AC: if using Cucumber/Playwright, tests write themselves from AC
    how
    • ·
      Each AC independently testable — do not chain multiple outcomes into one AC
    • ·
      Edge cases from INVEST size estimate: 'what are the boundaries of this story?'
    • ·
      Error paths mandatory: 'What does the user see when it fails?'
  6. 06
    Story dependencies
    Identify dependencies between stories and mark blockers.
    dependency graph
    what

    AI builds a dependency graph between stories.

    • 1
      3 dependency types
      • Hard blocker — story B cannot start until A is done
      • Soft dependency — B is easier if A is done, but B can still start
      • Independent — no dependency
    • 2
      AI identifies the critical path — longest blocker chain
    • 3
      Flag stories with >3 dependencies — signal must split or re-sequence
    why
    • ·
      Dependency graph drives sprint planning and parallel work
    • ·
      Critical path = minimum sprint duration — visualize it for the SM
    • ·
      Unresolved dependencies = sprint blocker surprise
    how
    • ·
      Build graph: nodes = stories, directed edges = blocker
    • ·
      Critical path analysis: longest path = minimum sprint duration
    • ·
      Parallel opportunities: stories with no outgoing dependencies = safe to parallelize
    • ·
      Cross-epic dependency = red flag: story depending on another epic breaks sprint boundaries
  7. 07
    Finalize epics.md
    Save all epics + nested stories into a single epics.md.
    backlog
    what

    AI save epics.md per epic — stories nested inside.

    • 1
      File structure
      • 1 file per epic: epic-01-user-onboarding.md
      • Frontmatter: epic ID, status, owner, WSJF score
      • Stories inside: S1, S2, S3... with full AC and technical context
    • 2
      Sharding for large epics
      • Option: stories/epic-01/story-01.md separated
      • Use when epic has >10 stories — avoid one huge file
    • 3
      AI invokes bmad-help → suggest Readiness Check (03·D) next
    why
    • ·
      1 file 1 epic = just-in-time loading — Phase 4 loads only 1 epic at a time
    • ·
      Full story content in the epic file = dev agent reads only the epic, no PRD/architecture required
    • ·
      Separation ensures minimal context window per sprint
    how
    • ·
      Frontmatter: epicId, status, owner, sequence, wsjfScore
    • ·
      Stories numbered S1, S2 within epic — globally unique via epic-01-S1
    • ·
      bmad-help recommend: Readiness Check (03·D) before Phase 4
Skills
bmad-create-epics-and-stories
Frameworks
INVESTStory SlicingAcceptance CriteriaDependency MappingEpic Ordering
Output Formats
Markdown epic-{N}.mdstories/*.md
Tools · MCP
party-mode (PM+Architect)bmad-help
IN
prd.mdARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
OUT
epics.mdstories/*.md
03 · F
CI/CD Pipeline
Scaffold a production-ready CI quality pipeline — parallel sharding (4 runners, fail-fast: false), burn-in flaky detection (10× iterations), dependency caching, artifact collection on failure, and quality gate enforcement (P0=100%, P1≥95%). Supports GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, Harness CI, and Circle CI. Prerequisite: test framework must be set up first and local tests must pass.
operated byTest Architect (TEA)
  1. 01
    Preflight checks
    Verify Git repo exists, detect stack, confirm test framework config is present, run local tests (halt with "Fix failing tests before CI setup" if they fail); detect CI platform from existing config files or git remote URL; default to GitHub Actions.
    platform detectionprerequisites
  2. 02
    Generate CI pipeline
    Select platform-specific template; configure all stages (lint → test → contract-test → burn-in → report); set up parallel sharding (4 shards via matrix strategy); route all user-controlled contexts through env: intermediaries to prevent script injection.
    parallel shardingpipeline stagesinjection prevention
  3. 03
    Configure quality gates & notifications
    Enable burn-in loop (10× iterations, frontend/fullstack by default; backend skipped as deterministic); set quality thresholds (P0=100%, P1≥95%); configure can-i-deploy contract gate if Pact is enabled; wire Slack/email failure notifications with artifact links.
    burn-in loopquality gatesP0/P1 thresholds
  4. 04
    Validate & summarize
    Validate generated config against the full checklist (config path, stages, sharding, burn-in, artifacts, secrets); fix any gaps before marking complete; write completion report with config path, enabled stages, required secrets, and next steps (set secrets, push, run pipeline).
    secrets checklistvalidation
Skills
bmad-testarch-ci
Frameworks
GitHub ActionsGitLab CIJenkinsAzure DevOpsHarness CICircle CI
Formats
.github/workflows/test.yml (platform path){test_artifacts}/ci-pipeline-progress.md
Tools · MCP
bmad-help
IN
framework config filespassing local tests
OUT
CI config file{test_artifacts}/ci-pipeline-progress.md
04
Implementation
Build one story at a time · Sprint-based loop

once

per epic

per story

TEA
per epic
Retrospective

04 · A
Sprint Planning
Initialize and refresh tracking — parse epics, detect story statuses from the file system, generate sprint-status.yaml.
run byDev
  1. 01
    Parse epic files
    Scan epic files, extract epic numbers and story IDs/titles from headers in the body.
    epic discovery
    what

    Scan *epic*.md in {planning_artifacts}, extract epic numbers and story IDs/titles from headers.

    • 1
      Find epic files — whole doc (epics.md) or sharded (index.md + section files)
    • 2
      Extract epic numbers from headers ## Epic 1:, story IDs and titles from ### Story 1.1: Title
    • 3
      Convert story header into kebab-case key: 1-1-user-authentication
    • 4
      Build complete inventory of all epics and stories
    why
    • ·
      Sprint planning needs ALL epics and stories to build complete status tracking
    how
    • ·
      Prefer the whole document over sharded files when both exist
    • ·
      Fuzzy matching: accept epics.md, bmm-epics.md, user-stories.md
    • ·
      Story ID: replace . with -, title into kebab-case
  2. 02
    Build status structure
    Create a flat development_status map with default statuses for epics, stories, retrospectives.
    flat mapdefault status
    what

    Build development_status map — each epic receives three entry types.

    • 1
      Epic entry: epic-{N}: backlog
    • 2
      Story entries: {epic}-{story}-{title}: backlog
    • 3
      Retrospective entry: epic-{N}-retrospective: optional
    how
    • ·
      Order: epic → that epic's stories → retrospective → next epic
    • ·
      Default backlog for epic and story; optional for retro
  3. 03
    Apply status detection
    Check actual story files, upgrade status when the file exists; never downgrade.
    file detectionnever downgrade
    what

    For each story, check {story_location}/{story-key}.md has exists not.

    • 1
      File exists → upgrade to at least ready-for-dev
    • 2
      Status current progress further → keep unchanged (not downgrade)
    • 3
      File does not exist and has no status yet → keep backlog
    how
    • ·
      Check path: {story_location}/{story-key}.md
    • ·
      Compare with existing status in sprint-status.yaml if file already exists at, take status progress further
  4. 04
    Generate sprint-status.yaml
    Write the flat development_status map with metadata header to file.
    artifact
    what

    Write sprint-status.yaml with flat development_status map and metadata header.

    how
    • ·
      Output path: {implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml
    • ·
      Metadata appears twice: format comment (#) to read, and format YAML key:value to parse
  5. 05
    Validate + Report
    Check completeness and report totals before ending.
    validation
    what

    Validate completeness and report totals.

    • 1
      Validate: every epic/story/retro has an entry, no extra entries exist, status values are valid, and YAML syntax is correct
    • 2
      Report totals: epic count, story count, in-progress count, done count
    why
    • ·
      Artifacts drive state — YAML persists across sessions, every agent read same one source
Skills
bmad-sprint-planning
Frameworks
Flat status mapFile-based status detectionNever-downgrade ruleStatus state machine
Formats
YAML sprint-status.yaml
IN
*epic*.md story files sprint-status.yaml
OUT
sprint-status.yaml
04 · B
Test Design TEA
Create epic-level test architecture and a risk-based coverage plan across all stories before development begins.
run byTEA
  1. 01
    Load epic context
    Read epic, architecture, project context, and story acceptance criteria.
    contextFR inventory
    what

    Read epic, architecture, project context, and story acceptance criteria.

    why

    Coverage must be anchored to actual requirements and architecture constraints.

    how

    Inventory functional requirements, scenarios, NFRs, and integration boundaries.

  2. 02
    Assess risk
    Score probability and impact for each scenario or requirement group.
    risk
    what

    Score probability and impact for each scenario or requirement group.

    why

    The highest-risk behavior deserves the strongest evidence.

    how

    Map risks into P0-P3 priorities and record the rationale.

  3. 03
    Choose test levels
    Assign unit, integration, contract, E2E, and NFR checks to the right risks.
    test pyramid
    what

    Assign unit, integration, contract, E2E, and NFR checks to the right risks.

    why

    A balanced pyramid gives confidence without creating a brittle suite.

    how

    Use unit tests for logic, integration for boundaries, E2E for critical journeys, and NFR checks for quality attributes.

  4. 04
    Generate coverage plan
    Write the per-story coverage strategy and evidence expectations.
    coverage plan
    what

    Write the per-story coverage strategy and evidence expectations.

    why

    The plan becomes the test architecture contract for ATDD, automation, and release traceability.

    how

    Document test levels, fixtures, data, priority, and owner.

  5. 05
    Validate and hand off
    Check for uncovered or untestable requirements and hand gaps to ATDD, Dev, or PM.
    handoff
    what

    Check for uncovered or untestable requirements and hand gaps to ATDD, Dev, or PM.

    why

    Gaps found here are cheaper than gaps found at release gate.

    how

    Flag missing ACs, ambiguous requirements, and coverage decisions needing human approval.

Skills
bmad-testarch-test-design
Frameworks
Risk scoring P0-P3Test pyramidCoverage planning
Formats
test-design-epic-{N}.md
Tools / MCP
bmad-help
IN
epics.mdARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdproject-context.md
OUT
test-design-epic-{N}.md
04 · C
ATDD TEA
Generate failing acceptance-test scaffolds from story acceptance criteria before production code is written.
run byTEA
  1. 01
    Read acceptance criteria
    Extract behaviors, examples, error paths, and observable outcomes.
    AC
    what

    Extract behaviors, examples, error paths, and observable outcomes.

    why

    ATDD must test user-visible behavior, not implementation guesses.

    how

    Map each AC to one or more executable examples.

  2. 02
    Write behavior scenarios
    Translate acceptance criteria into Given/When/Then or equivalent spec cases.
    scenario
    what

    Translate acceptance criteria into Given/When/Then or equivalent spec cases.

    why

    Scenario language exposes missing preconditions and expected outcomes.

    how

    Name scenarios after behavior, not functions.

  3. 03
    Generate failing scaffold
    Create tests, fixtures, and helper stubs without implementing production behavior.
    red phase
    what

    Create tests, fixtures, and helper stubs without implementing production behavior.

    why

    The failing state proves the test can detect missing behavior.

    how

    Avoid skipped tests and placeholder assertions.

  4. 04
    Verify red status
    Run the test runner and confirm failures are meaningful.
    verification
    what

    Run the test runner and confirm failures are meaningful.

    why

    A test that passes before code does not guide implementation.

    how

    Record failure output and any setup issues.

  5. 05
    Hand off to execution
    Attach test commands and expected red failures to the implementation plan.
    handoff
    what

    Attach test commands and expected red failures to the implementation plan.

    why

    Implementers need a clear target for green state.

    how

    Link tests to tasks and acceptance criteria.

Skills
bmad-testarch-atdd
Frameworks
Given/When/ThenRed phaseAcceptance criteria mapping
Formats
*.spec.ts*.feature
Tools / MCP
test runner
IN
story-[slug].mdtest-design-epic-{N}.md
OUT
tests/**/*.spec.ts
04 · B
Create Story
Create a comprehensive story file — exhaustive artifact analysis, architecture guardrails, web research latest tech — so the dev agent has everything needed for a clean implementation.
run byDev
  1. 01
    Determine target story
    The user provides a story path, or the first backlog entry is auto-discovered in sprint-status.yaml.
    story selection
    what

    Determine which story will be created: the user provides a story path (e.g. 1-2, epic 2 story 4), or the workflow auto-discovers it from sprint-status.yaml.

    • ·
      User provides → parse into story_key
    • ·
      Auto-discover: read FULL sprint-status.yaml (ALL lines, not skip) → find entry first match pattern N-N-name with status=backlog
    • ·
      If this is the first story in the epic, update epic status from backlog to in-progress
    • ·
      Halt if there is no backlog story
    why
    • ·
      Just-in-time: create-story runs per story, not as bulk prep
    • ·
      Bulk prep upfront makes later stories stale as new learnings change the context
    • ·
      ZERO USER INTERVENTION: fully automated except for the initial story selection
    how
    • ·
      Read FULL sprint-status.yaml — not skip any line any
    • ·
      Filter entries matching the N-N-name pattern, then take the first entry with status=backlog
    • ·
      Halt with message clearly if not find found backlog story
  2. 02
    Load and analyze core artifacts
    Load epics, PRD, architecture, and UX (SELECTIVE_LOAD); read the previous story file and analyze the latest git commits.
    previous story intelligencegit intelligence
    what

    Load all required core artifacts and analyze context from previous work.

    • ·
      Load: epics (*epic*.md), prd (*prd*.md), architecture (*architecture*.md), ux (*ux*.md) — SELECTIVE_LOAD
    • ·
      If story_num > 1: load previous story file → extract dev notes, review feedback, code patterns established, problems encountered
    • ·
      Analyze 1-5 git commits latest: files modified, patterns, dependencies, test approaches
    why
    • ·
      Previous story intelligence avoids reinventing the wheel and repeating mistakes
    • ·
      Git intelligence reveals the patterns and dependencies actually being used
    • ·
      SELECTIVE_LOAD keep context window focused — not dump entire artifacts
    how
    • ·
      Load epics in full; for other files, load only sections relevant to the story
    • ·
      Previous story: read dev notes and review feedback — extract patterns already is approve
    • ·
      Git: git log --oneline -5 → identify the files modified most often
  3. 03
    Architecture analysis for developer guardrails
    Extract tech stack, naming conventions, API patterns, DB schemas, and security requirements; fully read files marked UPDATE.
    architecture guardrailsregression prevention
    what

    Analyze architecture to create guardrails that keep the dev agent on the right path.

    • ·
      Extract: tech stack, code structure, naming conventions, API patterns, DB schemas, security requirements, performance requirements, testing standards, deployment patterns, integration patterns
    • ·
      Identify files marked UPDATE (not NEW) → read each file completely
    • ·
      Document: current state / what story changes / what must be preserved
    why
    • ·
      Skipping UPDATE files is the primary cause of implementation failures and review cycles
    • ·
      Architecture guardrails prevent common LLM mistakes: wrong libraries, wrong file locations, breaking regressions
    • ·
      Story implementation must leave system working end-to-end — not break existing functionality
    how
    • ·
      EXHAUSTIVE ANALYSIS REQUIRED: read fully, not skim
    • ·
      Read each UPDATE file completely — not just headers or summaries
    • ·
      Document clearly: what file X currently contains, what the story will change, and which parts must be preserved
  4. 04
    Web research for latest technical specifics
    Research latest stable versions, breaking changes, security vulnerabilities, deprecations, and best practices for libraries/frameworks in the story.
    web researchlatest tech
    what

    Identify libraries/frameworks from architecture analysis, then use web search to gather the latest technical information.

    • ·
      Latest stable version of each relevant library
    • ·
      Breaking changes, security vulnerabilities, deprecations
    • ·
      Best practices and migration notes current
    why
    • ·
      LLM training data may be outdated — web research ensures the dev uses correct, non-deprecated APIs
    • ·
      Security vulnerabilities in dependencies = risk if not check
    • ·
      Breaking changes in minor/patch versions can cause runtime errors
    how
    • ·
      Web search each library: "library-name latest version changelog"
    • ·
      Include in story: specific versions, API endpoints, security patches, migration notes
    • ·
      Research only libraries directly relevant to the story — not the entire stack
  5. 05
    Create comprehensive story file
    Initialize from the template, fully write all sections, and set Status = ready-for-dev. Save questions for the end; do not interrupt the workflow.
    story fileready-for-dev
    what

    Synthesize all analysis from steps 1-4 into a comprehensive story file at {implementation_artifacts}/{story_key}.md.

    • ·
      Initialize from template.md; write story requirements, developer context, technical requirements, and architecture compliance
    • ·
      Include: library/framework requirements, file structure, testing requirements
    • ·
      Include (if has): previous story intelligence, git intelligence, latest tech info from web research
    • ·
      Set Status = ready-for-dev; SAVE QUESTIONS for end — not interrupt workflow
    why
    • ·
      Story file = self-contained handoff artifact — dev agent does not need to re-read PRD/architecture/epics
    • ·
      ZERO USER INTERVENTION: do not ask the user midway; make decisions independently and note questions for the end
    • ·
      Comprehensive context means cleaner implementation and fewer review cycles
    how
    • ·
      Path: {implementation_artifacts}/{story_key}.md
    • ·
      Initialize from the template — do not write from scratch; fill each section in order
    • ·
      If there are questions, note them at the end of the story; do not stop to ask the user
  6. 06
    Update sprint status and finalize
    Validate story file, update sprint-status.yaml (status → ready-for-dev, last_updated), preserve ALL comments and structure, report completion.
    validationstatus update
    what

    Validate the story file and update sprint-status.yaml to signal the story is ready.

    • ·
      Validate story file against checklist.md → apply fixes if needs
    • ·
      Load FULL sprint-status.yaml, find story_key, verify status=backlog
    • ·
      Update: status → ready-for-dev, update last_updated
    • ·
      Save: preserve ALL comments and structure; report completion
    why
    • ·
      Validation ensures the story file is complete before dispatch, so the dev agent does not discover missing information later
    • ·
      Status ready-for-dev (not in-progress) clearly signals the story is prepared but not yet started
    • ·
      Preserve YAML comments because they contain important context for other team members
    how
    • ·
      Checklist validation: each required section is present, with no placeholder text
    • ·
      YAML update: only change status and last_updated — not rewrite entire file
    • ·
      Report the story name, path, and a summary of sections created
Skills
bmad-create-story
Frameworks
Just-in-time creationExhaustive artifact analysisPrevious story intelligenceArchitecture guardrails
Formats
Markdown story-[slug].mdYAML status frontmatter
IN
sprint-status.yaml *epic*.md *architecture*.md *prd*.md *ux*.md previous story file
OUT
{story_key}.md sprint-status.yaml
04 · C
Dev Story
Implement from the story file — auto-discover the story, run RED-GREEN-REFACTOR continuously for each task, validate DoD, and hand off for code review.
run byDev
  1. 01
    Find & load story
    Auto-discover the first story with status=ready-for-dev in sprint-status.yaml, or use the story path provided by the user; read the FULL story file.
    story discoverysprint-status
    what

    Determine which story needs implementation and read the entire content.

    • ·
      User provides story path → use it directly
    • ·
      No path → read FULL sprint-status.yaml → find the first N-N-name entry with status=ready-for-dev
    • ·
      Read COMPLETE story file: AC, Tasks/Subtasks, Dev Notes, Dev Agent Record, File List, Change Log, Status
    • ·
      Load project-context.md if exists
    • ·
      HALT if not find is story or story file not accessible
    why
    • ·
      Story file is the single source of truth — every implementation decision comes from there
    • ·
      Dev Notes contain architecture guardrails and previous learnings from create-story — skipping them means missing important context
    how
    • ·
      Read FULL sprint-status.yaml (ALL lines, do not skip) → filter N-N-name → take the first entry with status=ready-for-dev
    • ·
      If there is no ready-for-dev story, ask the user to run create-story, validate-create-story, or provide a story path
  2. 02
    Detect review continuation
    Check whether the story has already gone through code review; if so, extract pending follow-up items and prioritize them.
    review continuation
    what
    • ·
      Scan story file: has section "Senior Developer Review (AI)" not?
    • ·
      If yes → review_continuation = true: extract review outcome, count unchecked [AI-Review] items, and save the pending list
    • ·
      If not → review_continuation = false: fresh start
    why
    • ·
      Review follow-ups must be handled before regular tasks — the reviewer already identified concrete issues
    • ·
      Missing this detection can skip high-severity review findings
    how
    • ·
      Scan "Review Follow-ups (AI)" subsection in Tasks/Subtasks
    • ·
      Count High / Med / Low severity items still unchecked
  3. 03
    Mark in-progress
    Capture baseline_commit in story YAML frontmatter; update sprint-status.yaml: ready-for-dev → in-progress.
    baseline commitstate tracking
    what
    • ·
      Only perform this if current status = ready-for-dev AND story YAML does not yet have baseline_commit
    • ·
      Run git rev-parse HEAD → save to story YAML frontmatter: baseline_commit: <sha>
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: story_key → in-progress, update last_updated
    why
    • ·
      baseline_commit lets code-review know which commit the diff starts from
    • ·
      Status in-progress signals to the team that the story is being implemented
    how
    • ·
      If git not available → baseline_commit: NO_VCS
    • ·
      If YAML frontmatter already has baseline_commit → DO NOT overwrite
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: FULL file, preserve ALL comments and structure
  4. 04
    Implement task — RED·GREEN·REFACTOR loop
    Repeat for each task: write failing tests (RED) → implement minimal code (GREEN) → refactor → validate → mark [x] → next task. Do not stop midway.
    TDDred-green-refactorzero-interrupt
    what

    Per-task loop in the correct Tasks/Subtasks order in the story file:

    • ·
      RED: write failing tests → confirm tests FAIL before coding
    • ·
      GREEN: implement minimal code to pass tests — add nothing outside task scope
    • ·
      REFACTOR: improve code structure while tests remain green after refactor
    • ·
      Validate: run full test suite and check AC relevant to the task
    • ·
      Mark task [x] ONLY WHEN all tests actually exist and pass 100%
    • ·
      If review_continuation = true: handle [AI-Review] items before regular tasks
    why
    • ·
      TDD forces behavior articulation before choosing implementation — avoiding over-engineering
    • ·
      NEVER stop for "milestones", "significant progress", or "session boundaries" — only HALT when concrete conditions exist
    • ·
      NEVER mark [x] if tests have not actually passed — NO LYING OR CHEATING
    how
    • ·
      HALT if: needs an extra dependency outside the story spec, 3 consecutive implementation failures, or missing required config
    • ·
      Update File List after each task: ALL new/modified/deleted files (relative paths)
    • ·
      Write completion notes into Dev Agent Record after each task completes
  5. 05
    Story completion & DoD validation
    Verify all tasks [x], run full regression, validate Definition of Done, update status: in-progress → review.
    definition of doneregression
    what

    Final gate before the story enters the review queue:

    • ·
      Re-scan entire story document: verify ALL tasks and subtasks already [x]
    • ·
      Run full regression suite — not skip
    • ·
      DoD checklist: all AC satisfied, unit/integration/e2e tests added, linting passes, File List complete, Dev Agent Record has notes, Change Log has summary
    • ·
      Update story Status → review
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: story_key → review, preserve ALL comments
    why
    • ·
      DoD validation before signaling review avoids wasting reviewer time on incomplete stories
    • ·
      Status review (not done) waits for code-review approval before done
    how
    • ·
      HALT if: any task is not [x], regression failures exist, File List is missing, or DoD fails
    • ·
      YAML update: only change status and last_updated — not rewrite entire file
  6. 06
    Communication & handoff
    Report completion, explain according to user_skill_level, and suggest the next step: run code-review with a different LLM.
    handoff
    what
    • ·
      Summary: story ID, story key, title, key changes, tests added, files modified
    • ·
      Provide story file path and current status (review)
    • ·
      Ask whether the user needs any explanation (based on user_skill_level)
    • ·
      Suggest next steps: run code-review, verify ACs, check sprint status
    why
    • ·
      User needs to know the story is done and may have questions about implementation decisions
    • ·
      Code-review should use a different LLM to avoid blind spots from the same model that implemented it
    how
    • ·
      Tailor explanation to user_skill_level — juniors need examples, seniors need trade-offs
    • ·
      Tip: "For best results, run code-review using a different LLM than the one that implemented this story"
Skills
bmad-dev-story
Frameworks
TDD (test-first)Red-Green-RefactorDefinition of DoneZero-interrupt execution
Formats
Source codeTest filessprint-status.yaml
IN
sprint-status.yaml{story_key}.mdproject-context.md
OUT
source code + testssprint-status.yaml
04 · D
Code Review
Gather context through 5-tier cascade → spawn 3 parallel adversarial reviewers → triage findings → write into story file, resolve, update story status.
run byDev
  1. 01
    Gather context
    Find review target through 5-tier cascade; construct diff; verify determine review_mode: full (has spec/story file) or no-spec.
    5-tier cascadereview mode
    what

    Find review target through 5-tier cascade (stop at tier first find is):

    • ·
      Tier 1: explicit arg — PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, diff-mode keyword (staged/uncommitted/branch diff/commit range)
    • ·
      Tier 2: recent conversation context — scan a few recent messages
    • ·
      Tier 3: sprint-status.yaml → find story has status=review
    • ·
      Tier 4: current git state — if branch ≠ main, confirm with user
    • ·
      Tier 5: ask user directly
    • ·
      Set review_mode: full (has spec/story file) or no-spec
    why
    • ·
      Cascade avoids unnecessary user prompts — if it can find the target itself, it proceeds itself
    • ·
      review_mode=full → Acceptance Auditor run; no-spec → Acceptance Auditor is skip
    how
    • ·
      Construct diff: staged / uncommitted / branch diff / commit range / provided diff — verify it is non-empty before continuing
    • ·
      If diff > ~3000 lines → warn user, offer chunking
    • ·
      HALT + present summary (diff stats, review_mode, loaded docs) for user confirmation before review
  2. 02
    Parallel review — 3 subagents
    Spawn 3 adversarial reviewers in parallel, each agent receives different context.
    parallel reviewmulti-perspective
    what

    Dev spawn 3 parallel adversarial reviewer subagents, each agent receive context separate:

    • ·
      Blind Hunter: receives diff ONLY — no spec, no project context. Finds logic errors, security issues, runtime exceptions. Does not know intended behavior → catches unintended behaviors
    • ·
      Edge Case Hunter: receive diff + project read access. Trace all branching paths. Find: boundary errors, race conditions, null/empty/overflow
    • ·
      Acceptance Auditor: receives diff + spec file + context docs. Runs only when review_mode=full. Checks whether each AC is implemented, AC drift, and missing behaviors
    why
    • ·
      3 perspectives orthogonal — Blind Hunter: logic bugs; Edge Case: path gaps; Auditor: AC drift — not overlap, not miss
    • ·
      Single reviewer miss ≥1 dimension — multi-agent = better coverage
    how
    • ·
      All 3 run simultaneously (parallel), not sequentially
    • ·
      If subagents not available → generate prompt files, HALT; user run each file in session separate (ideally different LLM) and paste findings back
    • ·
      Layer fail or return empty → write into failed_layers, next continue with findings from layers remaining again
  3. 03
    Triage findings
    Normalize → deduplicate → classify into 4 buckets action dynamic: decision_needed, patch, defer, dismiss.
    normalizededuplicateclassify
    what

    AI automatically handles findings from 3 layers:

    • ·
      Normalize: convert output from 3 different formats (Blind Hunter: markdown; Edge Case: JSON; Auditor: markdown) into a unified format: id, source, title, detail, location
    • ·
      Deduplicate: same issue is 2-3 reviewers flag → merge into 1, set source = "blind+edge" v.v.
    • ·
      Classify each finding into correct 1 bucket: decision_needed (ambiguous, needs human), patch (unambiguous fix), defer (pre-existing, not required for this change), dismiss (noise/false positive)
    • ·
      Drop all dismiss findings — write count to the report
    why
    • ·
      Not dedup → human must sift through 3x noise
    • ·
      4 buckets action dynamic clearly than severity labels — each bucket has action specific can
    • ·
      Zero findings after triage → "✅ Clean review" — do not force minimum findings
    how
    • ·
      If review_mode=no-spec: finding any will is decision_needed then reclassify → patch (if fix clear) or defer
    • ·
      If failed_layers non-empty: warn user before announcing results
  4. 04
    Present, resolve & update status
    Write findings into story file; HALT to user resolve decision_needed + patches; update story status and sprint-status.yaml.
    story file updatestatus update
    what
    • ·
      Write findings into story file: append "Review Findings" subsection in Tasks/Subtasks with format - [ ] [Review][Decision/Patch/Defer] title
    • ·
      Write defer items into deferred-work.md
    • ·
      HALT: resolve decision_needed findings — user decide (into patch, defer, or dismiss)
    • ·
      HALT: handle reason patch findings — user choose: apply all / leave as action items / walk through each
    • ·
      Update story status: all resolved → done; remaining action items → in-progress
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: story_key → new status, preserve ALL comments
    why
    • ·
      Write into story file = findings persist in source of truth — not lost when session ending
    • ·
      decision_needed MUST have human input — AI must not decide ambiguous choices by itself
    • ·
      Status done only is set when all issues already resolved — not to story in limbo
    how
    • ·
      If no-spec: no story file → only present findings in chat, do not persist
    • ·
      If zero findings after triage → "✅ Clean review" → skip directly to status update
    • ·
      Suggest next: run dev-story (next story) or re-run code-review after fixes
Skills
bmad-code-review
Frameworks
Multi-agent Review (3 subagents)Adversarial Review4-bucket Triage
Formats
story file (Review Findings section)deferred-work.md
Tools · MCP
subagent spawning
IN
git diff (required)story-[slug].md (full mode)sprint-status.yaml (required)
OUT
story-[slug].md (findings appended)deferred-work.mdsprint-status.yaml
04 · G
Automate TEA
Expand test automation after implementation to cover edge cases, regressions, and critical journeys.
run byTEA
  1. 01
    Inspect existing coverage
    Read current tests, coverage reports, and changed files.
    coverage
    what

    Read current tests, coverage reports, and changed files.

    why

    Automation should fill real gaps, not duplicate existing evidence.

    how

    Map gaps to risk priorities from Test Design.

  2. 02
    Select automation targets
    Choose edge cases, regressions, integration points, and critical user journeys.
    targeting
    what

    Choose edge cases, regressions, integration points, and critical user journeys.

    why

    Not every path deserves E2E coverage.

    how

    Match test level to risk and maintenance cost.

  3. 03
    Generate tests
    Add automated tests with realistic fixtures and assertions.
    generate
    what

    Add automated tests with realistic fixtures and assertions.

    why

    Useful automation checks behavior and fails clearly.

    how

    Avoid brittle selectors and over-mocked integration behavior.

  4. 04
    Run and stabilize
    Run the suite repeatedly enough to catch flakiness.
    stabilize
    what

    Run the suite repeatedly enough to catch flakiness.

    why

    Flaky tests erode trust in CI.

    how

    Fix timing, data, and isolation issues before handoff.

  5. 05
    Update CI notes
    Document commands, test grouping, and CI configuration changes.
    CI
    what

    Document commands, test grouping, and CI configuration changes.

    why

    Automation only helps if the team can run it consistently.

    how

    Add config changes or explicit follow-up items.

Skills
bmad-testarch-automate
Frameworks
Regression expansionCoverage gap closureCI readiness
Formats
*.spec.tscoverage notes
Tools / MCP
test runnercoverage
IN
story-[slug].mdcoverage reportsexisting test files
OUT
expanded test suiteCI config notes
04 · RV
QA · Test Review TEA QA
